text: In SN2 reaction, the nucleophile attacks:
options: Same side as the leaving group
Opposite side (backside attack) to the leaving group
explanation: SN2: bimolecular, one-step, backside attack (180°). Causes Walden inversion (inversion of configuration at chiral centre).
name: SN2 Mechanism

options: Primary alkyl halides and strong nucleophiles
Tertiary alkyl halides and polar protic solvents
Secondary alkyl halides and polar aprotic solvents
explanation: SN1: favoured by 3° carbons (stable carbocation), polar protic solvents (stabilise ions), weak nucleophiles. Gives racemisation.
name: SN1 Conditions

text: The order of reactivity of alkyl halides toward SN2 reaction is:
explanation: SN2 reactivity: CH₃X > 1° > 2° > 3°. Steric hindrance increases from CH₃ to 3°, slowing bimolecular attack.
name: SN2 Reactivity Order

text: Aryl halides are less reactive than alkyl halides toward nucleophilic substitution because:
options: C–X bond is stronger in aryl halides
Resonance makes C–X bond shorter and stronger (partial double bond character)
explanation: In aryl halides, lone pairs on X overlap with aromatic π system → C–X has partial double bond character → C–X bond shorter/stronger → harder to break.
name: Aryl Halide Reactivity

text: The Finkelstein reaction involves exchange of halides using:
explanation: Finkelstein reaction: R–Cl + NaI (acetone) → R–I + NaCl. NaCl precipitates (insoluble in acetone), driving equilibrium forward.
name: Finkelstein Reaction

text: The Swartz reaction converts alkyl chlorides/bromides to:
options: Alkyl fluorides using CoF₂ or SbF₃
explanation: Swartz reaction: R–Cl + AgF → R–F + AgCl. Or with CoF₂, SbF₃. Used to make alkyl fluorides (direct fluorination is too vigorous).
name: Swartz Reaction

text: Reaction of alkyl halides with Mg in dry ether gives:
explanation: Grignard reagent: R–X + Mg → R–MgX (dry ether). RMgX is a powerful nucleophile/base used in organic synthesis.
name: Grignard Reagent

text: Wurtz reaction is used to synthesise:
options: Higher alkanes from alkyl halides + Na
explanation: Wurtz reaction: 2R–X + 2Na → R–R + 2NaX. Joins two alkyl groups. Best for symmetrical alkanes.
name: Wurtz Reaction

explanation: DDT: chlorinated insecticide, non-biodegradable (persists in environment, accumulates in food chain). Banned in most countries now.
name: DDT

text: The boiling point order among isomeric alkyl halides (same molecular formula) follows:
options: n > iso > neo (more branching = lower bp)
explanation: More branching → more compact molecule → weaker van der Waals forces → lower boiling point. n-alkyl > iso > neo.
name: BP Order of Alkyl Halides

text: In E2 elimination of alkyl halides, the preferred product (Saytzeff's rule) is:
options: Equal amounts of all possible alkenes
explanation: Saytzeff (Zaitsev) rule: major product of elimination is the more substituted (more stable, Hofmann rule is exception with bulky base) alkene.
name: Saytzeff Rule

text: The C–X bond energy order (bond strength) among halogens is:
explanation: C–F bond is strongest (short, due to small F). Bond energy: C–F(485) > C–Cl(327) > C–Br(285) > C–I(213) kJ/mol.
name: C–X Bond Energy Order

text: Nucleophilic aromatic substitution on aryl halides is facilitated by:
explanation: Nucleophilic aromatic substitution (SNAr): electron-withdrawing groups (–NO₂) ortho/para to leaving group stabilise the Meisenheimer complex intermediate.
name: Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution

text: Chloroform (CHCl₃) when oxidised in air slowly gives:
explanation: CHCl₃ oxidises in air to form phosgene (COCl₂), which is highly toxic. That's why CHCl₃ is stored in dark bottles with a little ethanol.
name: Oxidation of Chloroform

text: The reaction of alkyl halide with KOH/alcoholic gives:
explanation: KOH/alcoholic (strong base, non-nucleophilic solvent): favours elimination (E2). KOH/aqueous: favours substitution (SN2) → alcohol.
name: KOH Alcoholic vs Aqueous
